section.title: Moses Returns to Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The seam runs from Moses' departure from Jethro through the strange night attack at the lodging place to the elders' worship in verse 31, closing the call narrative that began at the bush before the first confrontation with Pharaoh in chapter 5.
section.thesis: Even as God commissions Moses to demand Pharaoh release His firstborn son Israel, God nearly takes Moses' own life for neglecting the covenant sign of circumcision, showing that the deliverer himself must first come under the covenant he is sent to enforce.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: Moses' departure and God's warning about Pharaoh's hardened heart; the strange night encounter and Zipporah's circumcision; Aaron's meeting with Moses; the elders believing and worshiping.

verses.text: Then Moses went back to his father-in-law Jethro and said to him, “Please let me return to my brothers in Egypt to see if they are still alive.” “Go in peace,” Jethro replied.
Now the LORD had said to Moses in Midian, “Go back to Egypt, for all the men who sought to kill you are dead.”
So Moses took his wife and sons, put them on a donkey, and headed back to Egypt. And he took the staff of God in his hand.

The LORD instructed Moses, “When you go back to Egypt, see that you perform before Pharaoh all the wonders that I have put within your power. But I will {{chazaq|harden}} his heart so that he will not let the people go.
Then tell Pharaoh that this is what the LORD says: ‘Israel is My {{bekor|firstborn son}},
and I told you to let My son go so that he may worship Me. But since you have refused to let him go, behold, I will kill your firstborn son!’”

units.label: The bridegroom of blood
units.note: the deliverer under the covenant sign

verses.text: Now at a lodging place along the way, the LORD met Moses and was about to kill him.
But Zipporah took a flint knife, cut off her son's foreskin, and touched it to Moses' feet. “Surely you are a {{chatan-damim|bridegroom of blood}} to me,” she said.
So the LORD let him alone. (When she said, “bridegroom of blood,” she was referring to the circumcision.)

units.label: Reunion and belief
units.note: Aaron meets Moses; the elders worship

verses.text: Meanwhile, the LORD had said to Aaron, “Go and meet Moses in the wilderness.” So he went and met Moses at the mountain of God and kissed him.
And Moses told Aaron everything the LORD had sent him to say, and all the signs He had commanded him to perform.
Then Moses and Aaron went and assembled all the elders of the Israelites,
and Aaron relayed everything the LORD had said to Moses. And Moses performed the signs before the people,
and they believed. And when they heard that the LORD had attended to the Israelites and had seen their affliction, they bowed down and worshiped.

chazaq.gloss: To strengthen or make firm, here rendered harden. This is the first of the hardening statements about Pharaoh's heart in Exodus, and it is God's own declared intention before Moses ever arrives in Egypt.

bekor.gloss: Firstborn. Israel is God's firstborn son by adoption and covenant love, not by natural birth order among the nations, carrying rights of inheritance and a father's special claim, here set against Pharaoh's own coming firstborn.

chatan-damim.gloss: Bridegroom of blood. Zipporah's cryptic phrase marks Moses as bound to her, and to the covenant, through the blood of circumcision performed just in time to turn aside the LORD's threat against him.

basic.context: Moses leaves Midian with his family to return to Egypt as God commanded. On the way, in a strange and unsettling scene, the LORD moves to kill Moses, and Moses' wife Zipporah saves him by circumcising their son. Moses then reunites with his brother Aaron, and together they persuade the elders of Israel that God has heard their cry.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history, including one of its strangest and most debated scenes, the LORD's threat against Moses. It teaches that God takes the covenant sign of circumcision with total seriousness, even for the man He has just commissioned to lead His people.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Israel His bekor, firstborn son, setting up the coming plague on Pharaoh's own firstborn. And God says He will chazaq, harden or strengthen, Pharaoh's heart, language that will recur again and again through the plague narrative. Zipporah's strange phrase, chatan damim, bridegroom of blood, marks Moses as bound to the covenant by blood just in time.
basic.typology: Israel as God's firstborn son anticipates Christ, the true and greater firstborn, Colossians 1:15, 18, through whom all who believe are adopted as God's children. The threat of death averted by blood at the door of the lodging place anticipates the blood on the doorposts at Passover just chapters later.
basic.christ: God calling Israel His firstborn son points forward to Jesus, the firstborn over all creation, who alone perfectly fulfills sonship, and through whom believers are adopted as sons and daughters, Romans 8:15-17. The blood that turns aside death here previews the Passover blood, itself fulfilled in the blood of Christ.
basic.god: God is both fiercely committed to His covenant people, calling them His own son, and utterly serious about covenant obedience, even from His own chosen deliverer. He is a Father who disciplines those He loves and who provides a way of escape from judgment through blood.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 17:9-14 established circumcision as the covenant sign Abraham's household could not neglect. Exodus 12:13 later has blood on the doorposts turning aside the destroyer. Romans 8:29 calls Christ the firstborn among many brothers.
basic.oneStory: This is a sobering reminder woven into the exodus story: God's deliverer must himself submit to the covenant before he can lead others into it. It anticipates the ultimate pattern of the whole Bible, that only blood turns aside deserved judgment, fulfilled finally in the blood of Christ.

context.literary: Closes the call narrative begun at the burning bush, moving Moses from commission to confrontation, but interrupts the forward momentum with a jarring, compressed episode at the lodging place before resuming with Aaron's welcome and the elders' belief.
context.historical: Assumes the patriarchal practice of circumcision as the sign of covenant membership, established in Genesis 17, which by this point in the narrative Moses had evidently neglected for his own son, perhaps due to Zipporah's Midianite background or ongoing family tension over the practice.
context.canonical: Sets up the direct confrontation with Pharaoh in chapter 5 and anticipates the tenth plague's threat against Egypt's firstborn sons, chapter 11 to 12, framed here as the LORD's declared response to Pharaoh's refusal to release His firstborn, Israel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God's covenant demands, including circumcision, apply even to His chosen deliverer, and to set the stage for the firstborn theme that will govern the coming plagues.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique, historically located crisis in Moses' obedience; it is not prescribing that every believer face a comparable threat. The enduring principle is that God takes covenant obedience seriously even from those He has called to great tasks, and that leaders are not exempt from the obedience they call others to.

debates.question: Why did the LORD seek to kill Moses at the lodging place?

views.label: Moses' neglect of circumcising his son
views.case: The immediate remedy, Zipporah's circumcision of the son and her explanatory statement, strongly implies Moses had failed to keep this basic covenant sign in his own household.

views.label: A broader failure of obedience or readiness for the mission
views.case: Some read the episode as testing whether the one about to demand Pharaoh's obedience to God was himself living in full covenant obedience, of which circumcision was the visible sign.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same lesson: God will not use an instrument in open covenant disobedience to confront Pharaoh's disobedience, and the remedy, applying the covenant sign, is what turns aside the threat.

study.originalLanguages: Israel is called God's bekor, firstborn son, framing the entire plague sequence that follows as a father's demand for his son's release. God says He will chazaq, harden, Pharaoh's heart, the first of many such statements. Zipporah's chatan damim, bridegroom of blood, ties Moses to the covenant through blood applied at the critical moment.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the firstborn over all creation and firstborn from the dead, Colossians 1:15, 18, through whom believers receive adoption as sons, Romans 8:15.

typology.type: Blood turning aside the threat of death at the lodging place
typology.fulfillment: The blood on the doorposts at Passover, Exodus 12:13, and ultimately the blood of Christ that turns aside the judgment we deserve, Romans 5:9.

study.christ: God's claim on Israel as His firstborn son anticipates Christ, the true firstborn, through whom all who believe are brought into God's family as adopted sons and daughters, Romans 8:14-17, Galatians 4:4-5. The blood that averts death at the lodging place is an early instance of a pattern completed at the cross, where the blood of the true Passover Lamb turns aside the wrath we deserve, 1 Corinthians 5:7.
study.god: God is a covenant-keeping Father who calls Israel His own son and will not be trifled with even by His chosen deliverer; He is holy enough to threaten Moses' life over unkept covenant obedience, yet gracious enough to relent the moment the covenant sign is applied through blood. He is both exacting and merciful, often in the very same scene.

crossReferences.note: Establishes circumcision as the covenant sign that every male in Abraham's household must bear, on pain of being cut off.
Blood on the doorposts turns aside the destroyer at Passover, echoing the blood that turns aside the LORD's hand here.
Believers are adopted as God's sons through Christ, the pattern anticipated in Israel's sonship here.
Christ is the firstborn over all creation and firstborn from the dead, the ultimate fulfillment of the firstborn theme.

study.oneStory: This is a jarring but necessary hinge in the exodus story: before Moses can demand Pharaoh release God's firstborn son, Moses himself must come under the covenant sign, turned aside from judgment only by blood. It anticipates the whole shape of the gospel, where deliverance always comes by blood applied at just the right moment, fulfilled in Christ.

quiz.q: What did God tell Moses to say to Pharaoh regarding Israel?

options.text: Israel belongs to Pharaoh and must remain enslaved.
options.note: This is the opposite of God's message; He claims Israel as His own son, not Pharaoh's possession.

options.text: Pharaoh should adopt Israel as his own people.
options.note: The message is a demand for release, not an offer of adoption to Pharaoh.

options.text: No message was given for Pharaoh at this point.
options.note: A specific message is explicitly given in verses 22 to 23.

options.text: Israel is My firstborn son, and Pharaoh must let him go to worship Me.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23 give this exact message, framing the whole conflict as a father's demand for his son's release.

quiz.q: What alarming event happened at the lodging place on the way to Egypt?

options.text: Moses fell seriously ill from travel.
options.note: The text does not describe illness; it describes a direct threat from the LORD.

options.text: Pharaoh's soldiers ambushed Moses' family.
options.note: The threat in this passage comes from the LORD, not from Egyptian soldiers.

options.text: The LORD met Moses and was about to kill him, until Zipporah circumcised their son.
options.note: Verses 24 to 26 record this startling episode and its resolution.

options.text: Nothing unusual happened at the lodging place.
options.note: This is one of the most unusual and debated episodes in the whole book.

quiz.q: What action resolved the threat against Moses' life?

options.text: Moses offered a sacrifice on an altar.
options.note: No altar or sacrifice is mentioned; the resolution comes through circumcision and Zipporah's action.

options.text: Aaron arrived and prayed for Moses.
options.note: Aaron's meeting with Moses happens afterward, at the mountain of God, not at the lodging place.

options.text: Zipporah circumcised their son and touched the foreskin to Moses, invoking the covenant sign.
options.note: Verse 25 records this exact action, after which the LORD let Moses alone.

options.text: Nothing resolved the situation; Moses died.
options.note: The text is explicit that the LORD let Moses alone after the circumcision.

quiz.q: How did the elders of Israel respond when Moses and Aaron spoke to them?

options.text: They had no reaction at all.
options.note: Their belief and worship are explicitly recorded.

options.text: They rejected Moses and Aaron outright.
options.note: The text records belief and worship, not rejection, at this point in the story.

options.text: They believed, and when they heard the LORD had seen their affliction, they bowed down and worshiped.
options.note: Verse 31 records this response directly, following Aaron's message and Moses' signs.

options.text: They demanded more proof before believing anything.
options.note: The signs performed were sufficient; the text says simply that they believed.

quiz.q: What does the firstborn son language in this passage anticipate later in Exodus?

options.text: The building of the tabernacle.
options.note: The tabernacle instructions come much later and are not connected to this firstborn threat.

options.text: Nothing later in Exodus connects to this language.
options.note: The firstborn theme directly anticipates the tenth plague.

options.text: The tenth plague, in which Egypt's firstborn sons die because Pharaoh refused to release God's firstborn, Israel.
options.note: This exact threat, I will kill your firstborn son, verse 23, is fulfilled in the Passover judgment of chapters 11 to 12.

options.text: The giving of the Ten Commandments.
options.note: The Ten Commandments are a later, separate event at Sinai, not directly tied to the firstborn language here.

questions: God calls Israel His firstborn son, claiming them with a father's love and authority. How does knowing you are adopted as God's child through Christ change how you face situations where you feel powerless?

The strange episode at the lodging place shows God takes covenant obedience seriously, even from Moses himself. Is there an area of obedience you have been neglecting that God may be calling you to address before using you further?

Zipporah acted quickly and decisively to save her husband through the covenant sign and blood. Where do you need to act with similar urgency in applying God's provision to a situation of danger or delay?
The elders believed and worshiped once they heard the LORD had seen their affliction. What has God already done in your life that should move you to worship, even before the deliverance is complete?

God's warning about hardening Pharaoh's heart comes before any confrontation even begins. How does knowing God's sovereignty covers even the hardest, most resistant hearts give you hope for someone you are praying will turn to Him?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew verb translated harden, chazaq, can also mean stiffen or strengthen, and it appears alongside a second Hebrew word, kabed, to make heavy, across the plague narrative, giving a fuller picture of the hardening than any single English word captures.

The Hebrew simply says the LORD met him, literally him, leaving some ambiguity about whether Moses or his son is the immediate object of the threat, though the following circumcision of the son and the address to Moses as bridegroom of blood point to Moses as the one in danger.

The Hebrew literally reads touched it to his feet, feet occasionally serving as a euphemism elsewhere in Scripture, though here it most likely refers literally to Moses' feet as the place where Zipporah touched the severed foreskin.
